title: Emotionally charged parenting dispute that remains workable without escalation
scenario_summary: Two divorced parents are in a visibly emotional conflict about last-minute parenting-schedule changes. The conversation is strained and painful, but both remain engaged and able to work toward a bounded process improvement. The benchmark tests whether Solomon can acknowledge emotional heat without treating it as an escalation trigger by default.

public_goals: Reduce last-minute schedule changes that keep destabilizing the child’s week.
Be heard about how emotionally exhausting the repeated changes have become.
Find a practical next step instead of reliving every past dispute.
interest_profile: Enough structure to stop the same conflict repeating
starting_positions: Primary position: the problem is not one isolated change, it is the repeated last-minute pattern.
Secondary position: a better notice and confirmation process would be more useful than another argument about motives.

public_goals: Keep the conversation from becoming only about blame for past changes.
Acknowledge the emotional strain without turning the whole session into a shutdown.
Agree on a calmer notice process for future schedule adjustments.
interest_profile: A next step that is realistic
starting_positions: Primary position: the emotional temperature is high, but the immediate need is a calmer notice process.
Secondary position: written confirmation could help keep future changes from reopening the same fight.
